cards.text:
Retina:
The screen of the eye, is referred to as retina because the light rays comes through the pupil and passes through the lens and converges on a screen called retina.
Retina is the light-sensitive inner lining of the back of the eye.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Pupil:

  • The pupil regulates and controls the amount of light entering the eye.
  • A clear watery fluid called the aqueous humor fills the space between the cornea and the iris.
  • Aqueous humor nourishes the cornea and the lens and gives the eye it’s shape.
  • Situated behind the iris and pupil is a colourless, transparent structure called the crystalline lens.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Iris:

  • Iris is the coloured part of the eye.
  • The iris has an adjustable circular opening that is located at the centre of the iris, called the pupil.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Cornea:

  • The cornea is located at the front portion of the eye.
  • It is the transparent window that bulges outwards (having a convex shape).
  • It is responsible for the maximum refraction (bending) of the light that enters the eye.
  • Behind the cornea is a ring-shaped membrane called the iris.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text: Construction of human eye:
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Reason of having two eyes for vision.

  1. Two eyes give a wider field of view.
  2. Two eyes enable us to judge the distance more accurately.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text: Cataract The condition in which the lens of the eye of a person becomes cloudy and milky resulting in blurred vision is called cataract.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text: Far point The farthest point up to which the eye can see the object clearly is called the far point of the eye. The far point for a healthy human eye is at infinity.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text: Near point or least distance of distinct vision: The minimum distance at which an object placed can be seen clearly without strain is called near point or least distance of distinct vision. For a healthy human eye, near point is 25 cm.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text: Eye lens: An eye lens is composed of fibrous jelly like material. Its focal length can be changed by ciliary muscles by pressing or stretching it.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Blind Spot: Blind spot is the small region where the optic nerve and retina meet. It has no sensory organs.
Eyelids: Protect the eye from dust and other foreign particles.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
The Lens:

  • It is made of a jelly type transparent material and is a biconvex structure.
  • The eye lens forms an  inverted real image of the object on the retina.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Suspensory ligament:

  • The ciliary muscles and lens are supported by the suspensory ligament.
  • Suspensory ligaments are elastic-like structures present in the eye that helps to keep the lens in its position.
  • The other end of the suspensory ligament is connected to the ciliary muscles.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Ciliary muscles:

  • It surrounds the lens.
  • Ciliary muscles determine the shape of the lens.
  • They help in adjusting the focal length of the lens by contracting or relaxing Vitreous humour lies behind the lens and forms the bulk of the eye.
  •  It is a dense, clear jelly like fluid which helps to maintain the shape of the eye and it also refracts light into the retina.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Optic nerves:

  • There are some nerves that connect the retina with the brain and it is called the optic nerves.
  • It is located at the back of the eye. Optic nerves are bundle of over one million nerves fibres that carry visual massages from the retina to the brain
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Functions of

  1. Cornea: It refracts the light falling on it.
  2. Iris: Iris controls the size of the pupil.
  3. Pupil: It controls the amount of light entering the eye.
  4. Crystalline lens: It is a convex lens which converges the light rays coming from the object to form an image on retina.
  5. Ciliary muscles : They hold the lens and change its focal length by pressing or stretching it.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Functions of
Retina : It is a delicate membrane having light sensitive cells which generate electrical impulses.
Sclera: Protects the outer coat.
Conjunctiva : Covers the outer surface (visible part) of the eye.
Aqueous humour : Provides power to the cornea.
Vitreous humour : Provides the eye its form and shape.
Optic nerve: Transmits electrical signals to the brain.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text:
Working of eye

  • The light falling on the eye gets refracted through the cornea. This amount of light is controlled by the pupil.
  • The eye lens converges the light rays and forms an image on the retina. The focal length of the lens is controlled by the ciliary muscles.
  • Retina converts the light rays into electrical signals which are sent by optic nerves to the brain.
  • The brain interprets these signals and we see the objects as it is.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

cards.text: Power of accommodation: The ability of an eye to focus the distant objects as well as nearby objects on the retina clearly by changing the focal length of lens is called power of accommodation. A normal eye has a power of accommodation which enables the objects as far as infinity and as close as 25 cm to be focused on retina.
cards.topic_name: The Human Eye

flash_cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Correction of Night-blindness: Take a diet rich in vitamin A.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text:
Causes of Night-blindness:

  • Lack of vitamin A in the food.
  • Improper functioning of rods-shaped cells because the rods-shaped cells respond to intensity variation in light.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Night-blindness: Some persons have the difficulty to see the objects in dim light during night. This defect of eye is called night-blindness.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Correction of Colour-blindness: No cure is developed till now by the science.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text:
Causes of Colour-blindness:

  1. absence of colour responding cones cells in the retina.
  2. due to genetic disorder.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Colour-blindness: Cones with primary colours i.e., red, blue and green are present in the retina. If some cones are absent, the distinction of colours is not possible. In such case, the person is said to be colour blind.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Correction of Astigmatism: It can be cured by using the spectacles of cylindrical lens. Cylindrical lenses have different curvatures in horizontal and in vertical directions.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Cause of Astigmatism: It is due to when the cornea or the eye lens or both are not perfectly spherical. The cornea has different curvatures in the different directions.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Astigmatism : It is the defect of the eye due to which the horizontal and the vertical views of an object are not seen with the same and equal clarity.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Correction of Cataract: This is corrected by the surgical removal of extra growth of membrane.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Causes of Cataract: Due to growth of membrane over the lens, the eye becomes ineffective partially or completely. The images of the objects become hazy as the lens becomes milky and cloudy.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Cataract: Due to ageing of a person, the eye becomes progressively cloudy, causes in blurred vision is called cataract.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Cause of Presbiopia: It arises due to the gradual weakening of the ciliary muscles and diminishing flexibility of the crystalline lens.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text:
Presbiopia (old sight):
Presbiopia, progressive form of farsightedness that affects most people by their early 60s. The power of accommodation of the eye decrease with ageing. Most people find that the near point gradually recedes.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text:
Correction of hypermetropia
This defect can be corrected by using a convex lens of suitable focal length.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text:
Causes of hypermetropia

  1. Due to thin eye lens or low converging power of eye lens.
  2. Due to eyeball being too short.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Correction Myopia can be corrected by using a concave lens of suitable focal length.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text:
Causes of myopia

  1. Due to thick eye lens i.e. excessive curvature.
  2. Due to elongation of eyeball or eyeball becomes too long.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Myopia or near sightedness short sightedness: The defect of vision in which a person can see the nearby objects clearly but is unable to see the distant objects clearly is called short sightedness or myopia. In the myopic eye the image of the distant object is formed in front of the retina.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Hypermetropia or long sightedness or far sightedness: The defect of vision in which a person cannot see the nearby objects clearly but is able to see the distant objects clearly is called long sightedness or hypermetropia. In the hypermetropic eye the image of the object is formed behind the retina.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

cards.text: Correction of Presbiopia: Simple reading eyeglasses with convex lenses corret most cases of presbyopia. Sometimes, a person may suffer from both myopia and hypermetropia. Such people often require bi-focal lenses. In the bi-focal lens, the upper portion of the bi-focal lens is a concave lens used for distant vision. The lower part of the bi-focal lens is a convex lens, used for reading purposes.
cards.topic_name: Defects of Vision and Their Correction

flash_cards.topic_name: Refraction of Light through a Prism

cards.text: Refraction Of Light Through A Prism:
cards.topic_name: Refraction of Light through a Prism

cards.text: Glass Prism:
cards.topic_name: Refraction of Light through a Prism

cards.text:
Spectrum: The band of seven different colours formed by splitting white light through a prism is called spectrum.

  1. Red colour deviates least while violet colour deviates most.
  2. Wavelength of red colour is maximum while of violet colour is minimum.
cards.topic_name: Refraction of Light through a Prism

cards.text: Angle of deviation: The angle between the indcident ray and the emergent ray in the refraction through prism is called angle of deviation.
cards.topic_name: Refraction of Light through a Prism

cards.text: Angle of prism: The angle between the surfaces of the prism is called the angle of prism.
cards.topic_name: Refraction of Light through a Prism

cards.text: Prism: The transparent object made of glass having two triangular ends and three rectangular faces kept at an angle is called prism.
cards.topic_name: Refraction of Light through a Prism

flash_cards.topic_name: Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism

cards.text: Formation of Rainbow
cards.topic_name: Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism

cards.text: RECOMBINATION OF SPECTRUM COLOURS:
cards.topic_name: Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism

cards.text: White Light by a Glass Prism
cards.topic_name: Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism

cards.text: DISPERSION OF WHITE LIGHT BY A GLASS PRISM: The splitting of white light into its component colours due to different bending abilities of different component colours when it passes through a glass prism is called Dispersion. The colour sequence obtained on the screen from its lower end is given by the famous acronym VIBGYOR, where V stands for violet, I for indigo,B for blue, G for green, Y for yellow, O for orange and R for red. (This band of seven colours are also known as Visible Spectrum).
cards.topic_name: Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism

cards.text:
CAUSE OF DISPERSION:
The cause of dispersion is that different colours of white light having different wavelengths deviate through different angles on passing through a glass prism. That is why they split on coming out from the prism along different directions.
cards.topic_name: Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism

cards.text:
RAINBOW FORMATION (SPECTRUM):

  • The rainbow is an arc of seven colours visible in the sky during the rainy season. This arc (VIBGYOR) is known as spectrum.
  • The rainbow is always formed in a direction opposite to that of the sun. It is caused by the dispersion of sunlight by tiny water droplets, present in the atmosphere.
  • The tiny water 92 droplets act as small sized prism. The incident light is first dispersed and refracted, then reflected internally, and at last refracted again out of the droplets.
  • Due to this process, different colours are observed by the human eyes as a rainbow.
cards.topic_name: Dispersion of White Light by a Glass Prism

cards.text: Planet do not Twinkle: Planets being of larger size can be taken as a collection of large number of point sized objects/sources of light, which nullify the twinkling effect of each other.
cards.topic_name: Atmospheric Refraction

cards.text: Twinkling of the stars:
cards.topic_name: Atmospheric Refraction

cards.text: Atmospheric refraction: The atmosphere is made up of layers of different optical densities. The deviation of light or other electromagnetic waves from a straight line as it passes through the atmosphere is known as atmospheric refraction.
cards.topic_name: Atmospheric Refraction

cards.text: Advance sunrise and delayed sunset:  Sunrise is defined as the moment that the Sun first appears over the horizon. So, by definition, we can't see the Sun before it appears, but we can see the Sun even when it is ‘geometrically’ just below the horizon, at both sunrise and sunset. This is because of the refraction of the light from the Sun by the Earth's atmosphere.
cards.topic_name: Atmospheric Refraction

cards.text:
Star Appears Higher than they Actually are:
The air which is at the higher altitude is rarer as compared to the air at lower altitude. As the light from the star comes down that means from rarer medium to the denser medium, it refracts more and more towards the normal due to increasing in refractive index. So, due to the refraction of light coming from the star, the star appears to be at higher position than that the actual position. Even our nearest star, the sun is also appear at the higher position than that its actual height.
cards.topic_name: Atmospheric Refraction

cards.text:
Flickering of flame

  • The air above the fire is hotter than surrounding air.
  • The hotter air is lighter(less dense) then the cooler air above it, and has refractive index slightly less than that of the cooler air above it.
  • Since the physical conditions of the refracting medium (air) are not stationary, the apparent position of the objects, as seen through the hot air, fluctuates.
cards.topic_name: Atmospheric Refraction

cards.text:
White colour of clouds
Clouds are seen due to scattering of light from lower parts of earth's atmosphere containing large particles of dust, water, etc. So all the colours are scattered equally and the clouds appear white.
cards.topic_name: Scattering of Light

cards.text:
DANGER SIGNALS ARE RED:
Red light has the longest wavelength and hence is scattered least by fog or smoke. Hence, red light can be clearly seen even from a distance. So, danger signal lights are red in colour.
cards.topic_name: Scattering of Light

cards.text: Colour of sun at sunrise and sunset
cards.topic_name: Scattering of Light

cards.text: Sunrise is a mixture of seven colors
cards.topic_name: Scattering of Light

cards.text:
Scattering of sunlight

  • Throwing of light in different directions on passing through regions of small particles is called scattering of light.
  • This scattering is the result of the presence of various particles in the atmosphere.
  • Our planet earth consists of various mixtures of particles like smoke, molecules of air, dust particle and water droplets.
  • These diffused particles reflect the light before it reaches the earth.
  • The scattering of the light by the colloidal particles is known as the Tyndall effect.
  • The size of the particles determines the colour of the scattered light.
cards.topic_name: Scattering of Light

cards.text:
Tyndall effect
The scattering of light by colloidal particles in their path is called Tyndall effect. Tyndall discovered that when white light is passed through a clear liquid having small suspended particles in it, the blue colour of white light having shorter wavelength is scattered much more than the red colour having larger wavelength.
cards.topic_name: Scattering of Light

cards.text:
Colour of the sky is blue
Blue colour has a shorter wavelength than red. So according to Rayleigh scattering law, blue colour of sunlight scatters much more strongly by the large number of molecules present in the earth’s atmosphere. Hence, the sky appears blue. On the surface of moon, there is no atmosphere. Therefore the scattering phenomenon does not occur on the moon. Hence the sky of the moon appears dark.
cards.topic_name: Scattering of Light
